title: 命案目睹记
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\136-命案目睹记-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Inspector Claddock discusses the difficulties of establishing an alibi with Wetherall, noting that a suspect might feign memory loss to conceal meetings with Dicky Rogers' associates. Claddock records several investigative theories in his notebook, considering whether the victim was Martina (the widow or girlfriend of Edmund Crakenesop), Anna Strawańska, or Harold’s first wife. He explores various motives, including bigamy, blackmail involving Alfred, or connections to Cedric’s time in Paris or the Balearic Islands. He also considers the possibility that the victim is disguised or that the killer is an unknown assailant. Claddock decides to inquire about a Christmas illness with Dr. Campbell, check Cedric's alibi, and consult Miss Marple regarding local rumors.

At number four Madison Road, Claddock visits Lucy Ashebrook and Miss Marple, paying Miss Marple a consultation fee of three pounds and three shillings. Claddock mentions that Sir Henry Clithering highly respects Miss Marple's understanding of human nature. Miss Marple explains her method of using character similarities to draw conclusions, comparing Harold to a conservative banker's son named Mr. Ides, Alfred to a slippery auto repairman named Jenkins, and Emma to a woman named Geraldine Webb. Lucy notes that Emma's brothers seem unsettled by her marriage, and Miss Marple suggests Emma might be a suitable match for the lonely Dr. Campbell. Lucy describes her exhausting efforts to find evidence at Rutherford Manor. Claddock reveals that while Harold remembers his whereabouts on December 20th of the previous year, Alfred does not. Claddock plans to speak with Cedric at Rutherford Hall and then locate Dr. Campbell.
